How did the Maryland case or record end?

Juvenile adjudication or offense committed as a minor

options: Adult non-conviction expungement under Crim. Proc. § 10-105

questions.helper: This answer is optional and does not select your legal pathway. The backend uses the factual answers and state rules.

questions.prompt: Ask in this order:
Was this an adult case or juvenile case?
Was there an arrest with no charge, or was a court case filed?
What court and county handled the case?
What is the case number?
What were all charges in the case or incident?
How did each charge end: guilty, PBJ, stet, nolle prosequi, dismissal, not guilty, acquittal, NCR, juvenile transfer, or something else?
Was any charge cannabis possession or cannabis possession with intent to distribute?
Was any charge a minor traffic violation?
Did any charge result in a guilty conviction?
Was the conviction one of the 10-110 listed offenses?
When was the sentence fully completed, including probation, parole, mandatory supervision, treatment, and restitution?
Do you have any pending criminal case now?
Were you convicted of a new crime during the waiting period?
Was the case domestically related?
Was any victim listed in the case?
Was the case pardoned, vacated, or based on conduct that is no longer a crime?
Do you need expungement, or would shielding be enough as a fallback?
How old were you when the offense or case began?
Have you completed every part of the sentence, supervision, probation, parole, classes, or community service?
Are all fines, restitution, court costs, and other required financial obligations resolved?
Do you currently have any pending criminal charge, open case, probation, parole, or other supervision?
Have you previously received record-clearing relief in Maryland or elsewhere?
Have you received a pardon, certificate of rehabilitation, or similar executive relief for this conviction?
What Maryland county, parish, borough, district, or local filing area handled the case?
Which court or agency handled the case?
What charge, statute, or record description appears on the record?
Does the record involve any Maryland category that the source identifies as excluded or review-required?
Do you have court paperwork or a criminal-history record showing the exact charge and outcome?
What case, docket, cause, or arrest number appears on the record?

pathways.label: Adult non-conviction expungement under Crim. Proc. § 10-105
pathways.summary: The broadest relief, available where the case did not end in a conviction: Acquittal, nolle prosequi, or dismissal — expungeable (note the 3-year rule below unless a tort waiver is filed). Probation before judgment (PBJ) — expungeable, generally 3 years after the PBJ (or on discharge, whichever is later), if no intervening conviction; a PBJ for a § 21-902 (Transportation) DUI/DWI carries a 15-year rule. Cannabis possession under Crim. Law § 5-601 — expungeable. Acts no longer crimes (§ 10-105(a)(11)) and convictions vacated under § 8-302 — expungeable, with a narrow carve-out for pre-2020 sodomy / pre-2023 § 3-322 offenses committed in…
ruleClauses: The broadest relief, available where the case did not end in a conviction:
Acquittal, nolle prosequi, or dismissal — expungeable (note the 3-year rule below unless a tort waiver is filed).

Cannabis possession under Crim. Law § 5-601 — expungeable.

Acts no longer crimes (§ 10-105(a)(11)) and convictions vacated under § 8-302 — expungeable, with a narrow carve-out for pre-2020 sodomy / pre-2023 § 3-322 offenses committed in aggravating circumstances.
Marylands most common expungement route is for cases that did not end in a regular conviction.
Eligible 10-105 outcomes include:
PBJ Expungement route unless excluded; waiting period applies
Nolle prosequi Expungement route; treatment nolle waits until treatment completion
Stet Expungement route; waiting period applies
Compromise under Criminal Law 3-207 Expungement route
Transfer to juvenile court Expungement route in court of original jurisdiction
Not criminally responsible for certain nuisance/trespass/disturbing peace/telephone misuse offenses Expungement route
Conviction for act no longer a crime Expungement route, but with important exceptions
Possession of cannabis under Criminal Law 5-601 Cannabis expungement route
Conviction vacated under actual innocence statute Expungement route
These categories are listed in Md. Criminal Procedure 10-105. (
"https://mgaleg.maryland.gov/mgawebsite/Laws/StatuteText?article=gcp&section=10-105")

PBJ for DUI/DWI under Transportation 21-902(a) or (b) 15 years after discharge from probation

Not criminally responsible route under 10-105(a)(9) or (10) 3 years after NCR finding
Cannabis possession conviction After sentence completion

Marylands automatic expungement applies to certain all-favorable cases after 3 years. It covers records relating to a qualifying civil cannabis offense or non-must-appear criminal charge when no charge in the case resulted in anything other than acquittal, dismissal, not guilty, or nolle prosequi, except treatment-related nolle prosequi is excluded. ("https://mgaleg.maryland.gov/mgawebsite/Laws/StatuteText?article=gcp&section=10-105.1")

Maryland Courts also states that for cases where all charges have acquittal, not guilty, dismissal, or nolle prosequi entered on or after October 1, 2021, the court will automatically expunge the case after 3 years; a person can request expungement earlier using CC-DC-CR-072C with no fee. ("https://www.mdcourts.gov/legalhelp/expungement")
Wilma rule: If the case qualifies for automatic expungement but the person wants faster relief before 3 years, route to CC-DC-CR-072C, not the ordinary 072A packet.
Most listed misdemeanors 5 years after completion of sentence
Second-degree assault / common law battery 7 years after completion of sentence
Most eligible felonies 7 years after completion of sentence
Possession with intent to distribute cannabis 3 years after completion of sentence
First-degree burglary, second-degree burglary, felony theft 10 years after completion of sentence
Domestically related crime 15 years after completion of sentence
exclusionRules: PBJ Expungement route unless excluded; waiting period applies
whether the PBJ is for an excluded offense category. ("https://mgaleg.maryland.gov/mgawebsite/Laws/StatuteText?article=gcp&section=10-105")

The unit rule. If one conviction arising from a single incident/charging document is NOT eligible, then NO conviction from that incident may be expunged. This is the most important Maryland-specific trap.

New conviction during the waiting period. A new conviction (other than a minor traffic offense) during the wait makes the original ineligible — unless the new conviction is itself eligible (the “chain reaction” rule).
Pending criminal proceeding. A person who is a defendant in a pending case is not eligible while it is pending.
Offense categories generally not expungeable
Convictions NOT on the § 10-110 enumerated list (most serious felonies, violent crimes, and offenses requiring sex-offender registration are excluded).
DUI/DWI convictions themselves rarely vanish; only outcomes short of conviction (e.g., a PBJ, with its 15-year rule) may be expunged.

The narrow § 10-105(a-1) carve-out: no expungement under the “no-longer-a-crime” provision for pre-October-2020 sodomy or pre-October-2023 § 3-322 offenses committed in certain aggravating circumstances (against a minor, by force, etc.).
Court debt is NOT a bar (2023). Unpaid court fees/costs do not bar expungement; the court must waive them — but this is separate from restitution.

Shielding limits. Once per lifetime; not for domestically related convictions; barred by an unresolved matter or a new conviction in the 3-year window (unless the new conviction is itself shieldable).
Out-of-state / federal records are not reachable by Maryland relief.
The § 10-110 eligible-offense list is long and enumerated by Criminal Law Article section, the unit
charge in the incident to its statute, apply the unit rule across the whole incident, and verify the
current waiting period and SB 432 status before declaring eligibility.
Always run the UNIT RULE first: pull every charge from the same incident and confirm each is eligible
DUI is the classic Maryland disappointment: a DUI conviction generally cannot be expunged; only a PBJ
or other non-conviction outcome can, and the DUI PBJ carries a 15-year wait.

pathways.label: Police-record expungement when no charge was filed under § 10-103
pathways.summary: 1 Get the full case record and every charge disposition. 2 Identify the court where the proceeding began, was transferred, or was appealed. 3 Pick the correct form: 072A, 072B, 072C, or 072D. 4 Add CC-DC-CR-078 General Waiver and Release if filing early for acquittal, dismissal, or nolle prosequi. 5 File in the court where the case was finally controlled by the statutes venue rule.
pathways.sourceRef: section:181-196; section:9-28; section:39-52; overview:9-28; corpus-unit:9; corpus-unit:29; corpus-unit:66; corpus-unit:4
ruleClauses: 1 Get the full case record and every charge disposition.
2 Identify the court where the proceeding began, was transferred, or was appealed.
3 Pick the correct form: 072A, 072B, 072C, or 072D.
4 Add CC-DC-CR-078 General Waiver and Release if filing early for acquittal, dismissal, or nolle prosequi.
5 File in the court where the case was finally controlled by the statutes venue rule.
6 Court serves the States Attorney.
7 For conviction expungement, court also notifies listed victims.
8 Wait for 30-day objection period.
9 If no objection, court may enter order.
10 If objection, attend hearing.
11 Keep the order and compliance certificates.

Maryland Peoples Law Library says petitions are generally filed in the court that heard/finally resolved the case, and separate courts require separate petitions. It also warns to use broad language requesting expungement of all police records, court records, and other Maryland State/local records related to the charge. ("https://www.peoples-law.org/how-do-i-file-petition-expungement")

For arrest/no-charge police records, start with the law-enforcement agency. If the agency denies the request or does not act after more than 60 days, use DC-CR-071 in court. ("https://www.mdcourts.gov/district/forms?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

pathways.label: Eligible conviction expungement under Crim. Proc. § 10-110
pathways.summary: Maryland allows expungement of many, but not all, guilty convictions. Wilma must match the exact charge statute against the Maryland Courts List of Expungeable Charges Under Criminal Procedure 10-110, currently form/list CC-DC-CR-072G2. pathways.label: Second Chance Act shielding
pathways.summary: Shielding is a fallback for certain convictions when expungement is unavailable or not yet available. It removes the record from public view but does not destroy it. ("https://www.peoples-law.org/shielding-your-convictions-public-maryland-second-chance-act?utm_source=chatgpt.com") Maryland Courts lists these eligible shielding convictions: failure to obey a reasonable and lawful order; malicious destruction of property in the lesser degree; possession or administering a controlled dangerous substance;
pathways.sourceRef: section:154-170; pathways:383-421; section:39-52; section:383-421; corpus-unit:19; corpus-unit:46
ruleClauses: Shielding is a fallback for certain convictions when expungement is unavailable or not yet available. It removes the record from public view but does not destroy it. ("https://www.peoples-law.org/shielding-your-convictions-public-maryland-second-chance-act?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Maryland Courts lists these eligible shielding convictions:
failure to obey a reasonable and lawful order;
malicious destruction of property in the lesser degree;
possession or administering a controlled dangerous substance;
possession or administering a non-controlled substance;
use of or possession with intent to use drug paraphernalia;
driving while privilege is canceled, suspended, refused, or revoked. ("https://www.mdcourts.gov/legalhelp/shieldingcriminalrecords?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

A person may petition for shielding no earlier than 3 years after satisfying the sentence for all convictions being shielded, including parole, probation, or mandatory supervision. A person may be granted only one shielding petition in a lifetime, and pending criminal proceedings block shielding eligibility. ("https://www.mdcourts.gov/district/selfhelp/secondchanceactshieldinguide?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

Wilma caution: Shielding is one-time-only. Do not recommend using it casually if the user has multiple counties or multiple future expungement possibilities. It may be strategically better to wait for expungement eligibility.
A lesser remedy that makes the record inaccessible to the public (and removes it from Maryland Judiciary Case

but leaves it visible to law enforcement, courts, and certain agencies. It applies to a list of about 12 “shieldable convictions” (excluding domestically related ones), is available no earlier than 3 years after the sentence is satisfied, and may be used only ONCE in a lifetime. Maryland's Clean Slate Act is set to automate shielding of eligible records.
Route by disposition. Non-conviction → § 10-105 expungement (the broadest). Eligible conviction → §
10-110 expungement after the REDEEM Act wait. If the conviction isn't § 10-110-eligible but is a
“shieldable conviction,” consider § 4-503 shielding — but it's once-per-lifetime and weaker.
Prefer expungement over shielding when both are available: expungement removes the record and
permits denial; shielding only hides it from the public while police and courts still see it.

Maryland courts explain expungement as removing information about a case from court and law-enforcement records, and warn people to keep copies because case records may be unavailable after expungement. (
"https://www.mdcourts.gov/legalhelp/expungement?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Path 1 — Expungement of charges, non-convictions (§ 10-105)
The broadest relief, available where the case did not end in a conviction:
Acquittal, nolle prosequi, or dismissal — expungeable (note the 3-year rule below unless a tort waiver is filed).

Probation before judgment (PBJ) — expungeable, generally 3 years after the PBJ (or on discharge, whichever is later), if no intervening conviction; a PBJ for a § 21-902 (Transportation) DUI/DWI carries a 15-year rule.
Cannabis possession under Crim. Law § 5-601 — expungeable.

Acts no longer crimes (§ 10-105(a)(11)) and convictions vacated under § 8-302 — expungeable, with a narrow carve-out for pre-2020 sodomy / pre-2023 § 3-322 offenses committed in aggravating circumstances.
Path 2 — Expungement of convictions (§ 10-110)
Available for an ENUMERATED list of convictions — over 100 misdemeanors (e.g., second-degree assault §
7-105, possession with intent to distribute § 5-602 with restrictions, and burglary in the first/second/third degree
Path 3 — Shielding (Pub. Safety § 4-503)

but leaves it visible to law enforcement, courts, and certain agencies. It applies to a list of about 12 “shieldable convictions” (excluding domestically related ones), is available no earlier than 3 years after the sentence is satisfied, and may be used only ONCE in a lifetime. Maryland's Clean Slate Act is set to automate shielding of eligible records.
Route by disposition. Non-conviction → § 10-105 expungement (the broadest). Eligible conviction → §
10-110 expungement after the REDEEM Act wait. If the conviction isn't § 10-110-eligible but is a
“shieldable conviction,” consider § 4-503 shielding — but it's once-per-lifetime and weaker.
Prefer expungement over shielding when both are available: expungement removes the record and
permits denial; shielding only hides it from the public while police and courts still see it.

waitingPeriodRules.ruleText: How the agent should use this document Maryland has three record-clearing paths and the agent routes by disposition and goal. (1) Expungement of charges (§ 10-105) clears NON-convictions — acquittal, nolle prosequi, dismissal, probation before judgment (PBJ), and cannabis possession — and is the broadest relief. (2) Expungement of convictions (§ 10-110) clears an enumerated list of misdemeanors and a handful of felonies after a waiting period. (3) Shielding (§ 4-503) is a lesser, once-per-lifetime remedy that hides a limited set of convictions from public view but not from law enforcement.

exclusionRules.ruleText: Eligibility is offense- and record-specific, and three structural rules in § 10-110(d) do a lot of the work.
Structural disqualifiers (§ 10-110(d))

The unit rule. If one conviction arising from a single incident/charging document is NOT eligible, then NO conviction from that incident may be expunged. This is the most important Maryland-specific trap.

New conviction during the waiting period. A new conviction (other than a minor traffic offense) during the wait makes the original ineligible — unless the new conviction is itself eligible (the “chain reaction” rule).
Pending criminal proceeding. A person who is a defendant in a pending case is not eligible while it is pending.
Offense categories generally not expungeable
Convictions NOT on the § 10-110 enumerated list (most serious felonies, violent crimes, and offenses requiring sex-offender registration are excluded).
DUI/DWI convictions themselves rarely vanish; only outcomes short of conviction (e.g., a PBJ, with its 15-year rule) may be expunged.

The narrow § 10-105(a-1) carve-out: no expungement under the “no-longer-a-crime” provision for pre-October-2020 sodomy or pre-October-2023 § 3-322 offenses committed in certain aggravating circumstances (against a minor, by force, etc.).
Restitution (2025 SB 432, § 10-110(f)(2)(v)). The court must find the applicant paid court-ordered restitution or cannot pay it before granting; unpaid restitution can block expungement.
Court debt is NOT a bar (2023). Unpaid court fees/costs do not bar expungement; the court must waive them — but this is separate from restitution.

Shielding limits. Once per lifetime; not for domestically related convictions; barred by an unresolved matter or a new conviction in the 3-year window (unless the new conviction is itself shieldable).
Out-of-state / federal records are not reachable by Maryland relief.
The § 10-110 eligible-offense list is long and enumerated by Criminal Law Article section, the unit
rule can disqualify an entire incident based on one charge, and the 2025 SB 432 restitution rule
and probation-violation fix are phasing in from October 1, 2025. The agent must map every
charge in the incident to its statute, apply the unit rule across the whole incident, and verify the
current waiting period and SB 432 status before declaring eligibility.

sourceSections.title: Remedy definitions Wilma must understand
sourceSections.text:
Expungement
Maryland expungement removes eligible court records, police records, and other qualifying State or local records from public inspection. The order can reach both court and police records, and custodians generally must certify compliance within 60 days after the order unless a statutory exception applies. ("https://mgaleg.maryland.gov/mgawebsite/Laws/StatuteText?article=gcp&section=10-105")
Automatic expungement
For qualifying cases entered on or after October 1, 2021, Maryland automatically expunges certain all-favorable-disposition cases after 3 years. The automatic route applies when no charge in the case resulted in anything other than acquittal, dismissal, not guilty, or nolle prosequi, except a treatment-related nolle is excluded. ("https://mgaleg.maryland.gov/mgawebsite/Laws/StatuteText?article=gcp&section=10-105.1")
Shielding
Shielding is not expungement. It makes certain conviction records inaccessible to the public, but does not destroy them. Marylands shielding statute defines
shield
as making a court and police record relating to a conviction inaccessible to members of the public. ("https://mgaleg.maryland.gov/mgawebsite/Laws/StatuteText?article=gcp&section=10-301&utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma rule: For Maryland, use expungement when the statute permits removal of records. Use shielding only as a fallback when the user has one of the limited Second Chance Act convictions and cannot get expungement.

sourceSections.title: Adult non-conviction expungement - 10-105
sourceSections.text:
Marylands most common expungement route is for cases that did not end in a regular conviction.
Eligible 10-105 outcomes include:

Outcome Wilma treatment
Acquittal Expungement route
Dismissal Expungement route
PBJ Expungement route unless excluded; waiting period applies
Nolle prosequi Expungement route; treatment nolle waits until treatment completion
Stet Expungement route; waiting period applies
Compromise under Criminal Law 3-207 Expungement route
Transfer to juvenile court Expungement route in court of original jurisdiction
Not criminally responsible for certain nuisance/trespass/disturbing peace/telephone misuse offenses Expungement route
Conviction for act no longer a crime Expungement route, but with important exceptions
Possession of cannabis under Criminal Law 5-601 Cannabis expungement route
Conviction vacated under actual innocence statute Expungement route
These categories are listed in Md. Criminal Procedure 10-105. (

"https://mgaleg.maryland.gov/mgawebsite/Laws/StatuteText?article=gcp&section=10-105")
10-105 waiting periods

Route Timing
Acquittal, nolle prosequi, dismissal 3 years after disposition, unless the person files a General Waiver and Release of tort claims arising from the charge
PBJ Later of discharge from probation or 3 years after PBJ was granted
PBJ for DUI/DWI under Transportation 21-902(a) or (b) 15 years after discharge from probation
Treatment nolle After required treatment is completed
Stet / compromise 3 years after stet or compromise
Nuisance/transit-type convictions listed in 10-105(a)(9) 3 years after sentence completion
Not criminally responsible route under 10-105(a)(9) or (10) 3 years after NCR finding
Cannabis possession conviction After sentence completion
Good cause Court may grant 10-105 expungement at any time on a showing of good cause
The timing rules above come from 10-105(c). (

"https://mgaleg.maryland.gov/mgawebsite/Laws/StatuteText?article=gcp&section=10-105")
10-105 bars
Wilma must check:
pending criminal proceeding;
PBJ followed by a new conviction within the relevant 3-year period;
DUI/DWI PBJ followed by new conviction or another DUI/DWI PBJ within the 15-year period;
whether the user owes restitution or can show inability to pay;
whether the case is blocked by the unit rule;
whether the PBJ is for an excluded offense category. ("https://mgaleg.maryland.gov/mgawebsite/Laws/StatuteText?article=gcp&section=10-105")
Wilma caution: A PBJ is not automatically ready for expungement. Wilma needs the PBJ date, probation discharge date, offense type, whether it was DUI-related, and whether there were new convictions.

sourceSections.title: Automatic expungement - 10-105.1
sourceSections.text:
Marylands automatic expungement applies to certain all-favorable cases after 3 years. It covers records relating to a qualifying civil cannabis offense or non-must-appear criminal charge when no charge in the case resulted in anything other than acquittal, dismissal, not guilty, or nolle prosequi, except treatment-related nolle prosequi is excluded. ("https://mgaleg.maryland.gov/mgawebsite/Laws/StatuteText?article=gcp&section=10-105.1")
Maryland Courts also states that for cases where all charges have acquittal, not guilty, dismissal, or nolle prosequi entered on or after October 1, 2021, the court will automatically expunge the case after 3 years; a person can request expungement earlier using CC-DC-CR-072C with no fee. ("https://www.mdcourts.gov/legalhelp/expungement")
Wilma rule: If the case qualifies for automatic expungement but the person wants faster relief before 3 years, route to CC-DC-CR-072C, not the ordinary 072A packet.

Cannabis-specific Maryland routing

Cannabis deserves its own Wilma branch.

Cannabis outcome Route
Cannabis possession under Criminal Law 5-601 Form CC-DC-CR-072D; petition after sentence completion
Possession with intent to distribute cannabis Form CC-DC-CR-072D; 3 years after sentence completion
Cannabis charge in same unit as other charges Cannabis possession is excluded from the unit rule, so it does not block expungement of the other unit charges
Governor cannabis pardon Pardon may shield or support relief, but full expungement still requires the correct expungement route
Maryland Courts lists CC-DC-CR-072D for marijuana/cannabis-related expungement, and Maryland Peoples Law Library states that cannabis possession with intent to distribute may be expunged 3 years after satisfactory completion of sentence. (

"https://www.courts.state.md.us/video/selfhelp/expungement-series-cannabis?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma caution: Do not collapse all cannabis cases into one rule. Possession, possession with intent to distribute cannabis, distribution of controlled dangerous substance, paraphernalia, and mixed-charge cases need separate review.

sourceSections.title: Shielding under Maryland Second Chance Act
Juvenile expungement
Filing workflow
sourceSections.text:
Court expungement
1 Get the full case record and every charge disposition.
2 Identify the court where the proceeding began, was transferred, or was appealed.
3 Pick the correct form: 072A, 072B, 072C, or 072D.
4 Add CC-DC-CR-078 General Waiver and Release if filing early for acquittal, dismissal, or nolle prosequi.
5 File in the court where the case was finally controlled by the statutes venue rule.
6 Court serves the States Attorney.
7 For conviction expungement, court also notifies listed victims.
8 Wait for 30-day objection period.
9 If no objection, court may enter order.
10 If objection, attend hearing.
11 Keep the order and compliance certificates.
Maryland Peoples Law Library says petitions are generally filed in the court that heard/finally resolved the case, and separate courts require separate petitions. It also warns to use broad language requesting expungement of all police records, court records, and other Maryland State/local records related to the charge. ("https://www.peoples-law.org/how-do-i-file-petition-expungement")
Police-record expungement
For arrest/no-charge police records, start with the law-enforcement agency. If the agency denies the request or does not act after more than 60 days, use DC-CR-071 in court. ("https://www.mdcourts.gov/district/forms?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

sourceSections.text:
For Maryland, train Wilma with this core logic:
Maryland has real expungement, but the unit rule controls everything.Non-convictions usually route through 10-105.All-favorable post-Oct. 1, 2021 cases may automatically expunge after 3 years.PBJ, stet, treatment dispositions, and DUI/DWI PBJ have special waits.Convictions route through 10-110 only if the exact statute is on the eligible list.Most listed misdemeanors are 5 years; assault/battery and most eligible felonies are 7 years; burglary/theft felonies are 10 years; domestically related crimes are 15 years.Cannabis has its own form and rules.Shielding is a fallback, not expungement, and can usually be used only once.Juvenile expungement is separate and has no filing fee.

===== SOURCE FILE: Maryland-Expungement-Shielding-Agent-Reference.pdf =====

Maryland Expungement & Shielding
Agent Training Reference
A complete operational knowledge base for clearing criminal records in Maryland:
expungement of charges (§ 10-105) and convictions (§ 10-110), the separate shielding
remedy (§ 4-503), the REDEEM Act waiting periods, the 2025 Expungement Reform Act,
eligibility, exclusions, procedure, and record requests.

Coverage State of Maryland (District or Circuit Court where the case concluded; MDEC e-filing)

Primary statutes Crim. Proc. § 10-105 (charges) & § 10-110 (convictions); Pub. Safety § 4-503 (shielding)

Controlling changes REDEEM Act (SB-37, 2023) reduced waits; Expungement Reform Act (SB 432, 2025) phasing from 10/1/2025

Currency Reflects law as of mid-2026, incl. SB 432 and the Clean Slate shielding automation

VERIFY BEFORE RELYING
Maryland offers TWO different remedies and the agent must not conflate them. EXPUNGEMENT
(Crim. Proc. §§ 10-105 / 10-110) removes the record so thoroughly that the person can usually
deny it ever happened. SHIELDING (Pub. Safety § 4-503) only hides the record from the public,
employers, and landlords — police, courts, and certain agencies still see it, and it is a
once-per-lifetime remedy for a limited list of convictions. Maryland's waiting periods and
eligibility changed materially under the REDEEM Act (2023) and the Expungement Reform Act of
2025 (SB 432), which phases in from October 1, 2025, and the Clean Slate Act is automating
shielding. The agent must use the correct remedy term and verify the current waiting period and
eligibility before relying on any rule.

How the agent should use this document
Maryland has three record-clearing paths and the agent routes by disposition and goal. (1) Expungement of
charges (§ 10-105) clears NON-convictions — acquittal, nolle prosequi, dismissal, probation before judgment
(PBJ), and cannabis possession — and is the broadest relief. (2) Expungement of convictions (§ 10-110) clears
an enumerated list of misdemeanors and a handful of felonies after a waiting period. (3) Shielding (§ 4-503) is a
lesser, once-per-lifetime remedy that hides a limited set of convictions from public view but not from law
enforcement. The agent should (1) determine conviction vs. non-conviction; (2) for non-convictions apply §
10-105 and its PBJ / 3-year rules; (3) for convictions check the § 10-110 eligible list and REDEEM Act waiting
period, then consider shielding if expungement is unavailable; and (4) always apply the UNIT RULE — if one
offense in an incident is ineligible, the whole incident is.

CONTENTS

sourceSections.title: Eligibility rules
sourceSections.text:
Three paths, considered by disposition and goal.

Path 1 — Expungement of charges, non-convictions (§ 10-105)
The broadest relief, available where the case did not end in a conviction:

• Acquittal, nolle prosequi, or dismissal — expungeable (note the 3-year rule below unless a tort waiver is
filed).
• Probation before judgment (PBJ) — expungeable, generally 3 years after the PBJ (or on discharge,
whichever is later), if no intervening conviction; a PBJ for a § 21-902 (Transportation) DUI/DWI carries a
15-year rule.
• Cannabis possession under Crim. Law § 5-601 — expungeable.
• Acts no longer crimes (§ 10-105(a)(11)) and convictions vacated under § 8-302 — expungeable, with a
narrow carve-out for pre-2020 sodomy / pre-2023 § 3-322 offenses committed in aggravating circumstances.

Path 2 — Expungement of convictions (§ 10-110)
Available for an ENUMERATED list of convictions — over 100 misdemeanors (e.g., second-degree assault §
3-203, drug possession, theft, prostitution, disorderly conduct, many fraud and regulatory offenses;
fourth-degree burglary was added in 2021) and a small set of felonies (theft § 7-104, motor-vehicle theft §
7-105, possession with intent to distribute § 5-602 with restrictions, and burglary in the first/second/third degree
§ 6-202(a) / § 6-203). After the waiting period (Section 2), the person files a petition; the court serves the State's
Attorney and notifies any listed victims.

Path 3 — Shielding (Pub. Safety § 4-503)
A lesser remedy that makes the record inaccessible to the public (and removes it from Maryland Judiciary Case
Search) but leaves it visible to law enforcement, courts, and certain agencies. It applies to a list of about 12
“shieldable convictions” (excluding domestically related ones), is available no earlier than 3 years after the
sentence is satisfied, and may be used only ONCE in a lifetime. Maryland's Clean Slate Act is set to automate
shielding of eligible records.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Route by disposition. Non-conviction → § 10-105 expungement (the broadest). Eligible conviction → §
10-110 expungement after the REDEEM Act wait. If the conviction isn't § 10-110-eligible but is a
“shieldable conviction,” consider § 4-503 shielding — but it's once-per-lifetime and weaker.
Prefer expungement over shielding when both are available: expungement removes the record and
permits denial; shielding only hides it from the public while police and courts still see it.

Maryland Expungement & Shielding — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Waiting periods
sourceSections.text:
Conviction waits run from satisfaction of the sentence (including parole, probation, or mandatory supervision).
The REDEEM Act (2023) cut most of them.

Disposition / offense Remedy Waiting period Notes

Acquittal / nolle prosequi / § 10-105 3 years (or sooner From disposition
dismissal with tort waiver)

Probation before judgment § 10-105 3 years (or on No intervening conviction
(PBJ) discharge, later)

PBJ for § 21-902 DUI/DWI § 10-105 15 years Transportation Article

Cannabis possession § 10-105 On petition Per statute
(§ 5-601)

Most eligible misdemeanors § 10-110 5 years REDEEM Act (was 10)

2nd-degree assault § 3-203 / § 10-110 Own period Per § 10-110(c)(2)
common-law battery

PWID cannabis § 5-602 § 10-110 3 years Special short wait

Eligible felonies: burglary § 10-110 10 years § 10-110(c)(6)
§ 6-202(a)/§ 6-203, theft § 7-104

Other eligible felonies § 10-110 7 years REDEEM Act (was 15)

Domestic violence crimes § 10-110 15 years Retained

Shieldable conviction § 4-503 3 years Once per lifetime

The REDEEM Act is the key recent change: most misdemeanor expungement waits dropped from 10 years to 5,
and eligible felony waits from 15 years to 7 (or 10 for the burglary § 6-202(a)/§ 6-203 and felony theft § 7-104
group), while the 15-year wait was retained for crimes of domestic violence. All conviction waits run from
completion of the entire sentence including supervision — outstanding supervision means the clock has not
started.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Compute conviction waits from satisfaction of the FULL sentence including parole/probation/mandatory
supervision. Most misdemeanors = 5 yrs; eligible felonies = 7 yrs, or 10 for burglary §6-202(a)/6-203 and
felony theft §7-104; domestic-violence = 15 yrs.
For non-convictions, the 3-year wait on acquittal/nolle/dismissal can be skipped if the person files a
written waiver and release of tort claims — flag that option.

Maryland Expungement & Shielding — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Disqualifying / ineligible offenses
sourceSections.text:
Eligibility is offense- and record-specific, and three structural rules in § 10-110(d) do a lot of the work.

Structural disqualifiers (§ 10-110(d))
• The unit rule. If one conviction arising from a single incident/charging document is NOT eligible, then NO
conviction from that incident may be expunged. This is the most important Maryland-specific trap.
• New conviction during the waiting period. A new conviction (other than a minor traffic offense) during the
wait makes the original ineligible — unless the new conviction is itself eligible (the “chain reaction” rule).
• Pending criminal proceeding. A person who is a defendant in a pending case is not eligible while it is
pending.

Offense categories generally not expungeable
• Convictions NOT on the § 10-110 enumerated list (most serious felonies, violent crimes, and offenses
requiring sex-offender registration are excluded).
• DUI/DWI convictions themselves rarely vanish; only outcomes short of conviction (e.g., a PBJ, with its
15-year rule) may be expunged.
• The narrow § 10-105(a-1) carve-out: no expungement under the “no-longer-a-crime” provision for
pre-October-2020 sodomy or pre-October-2023 § 3-322 offenses committed in certain aggravating
circumstances (against a minor, by force, etc.).

Other limits
• Restitution (2025 SB 432, § 10-110(f)(2)(v)). The court must find the applicant paid court-ordered
restitution or cannot pay it before granting; unpaid restitution can block expungement.
• Court debt is NOT a bar (2023). Unpaid court fees/costs do not bar expungement; the court must waive
them — but this is separate from restitution.
• Shielding limits. Once per lifetime; not for domestically related convictions; barred by an unresolved matter
or a new conviction in the 3-year window (unless the new conviction is itself shieldable).
• Out-of-state / federal records are not reachable by Maryland relief.

VERIFY BEFORE RELYING
The § 10-110 eligible-offense list is long and enumerated by Criminal Law Article section, the unit
rule can disqualify an entire incident based on one charge, and the 2025 SB 432 restitution rule
and probation-violation fix are phasing in from October 1, 2025. The agent must map every
charge in the incident to its statute, apply the unit rule across the whole incident, and verify the
current waiting period and SB 432 status before declaring eligibility.

Maryland Expungement & Shielding — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

AGENT GUIDANCE
Always run the UNIT RULE first: pull every charge from the same incident and confirm each is eligible —
a single ineligible charge sinks the whole incident.
DUI is the classic Maryland disappointment: a DUI conviction generally cannot be expunged; only a PBJ
or other non-conviction outcome can, and the DUI PBJ carries a 15-year wait.

Maryland Expungement & Shielding — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.text:
Disposition determines the remedy, the waiting period, and the procedure. This table is the agent's primary
routing logic for Maryland.

conviction

Effect of relief: an EXPUNGEMENT removes the record from public and government indexes — the person
can generally deny the arrest or conviction occurred. SHIELDING only blocks public access (and removes the
case from Judiciary Case Search) while law enforcement, courts, and certain agencies retain access, and the
person generally cannot deny it. SB 432 (2025) also: (a) clarifies a probation violation does not automatically
disqualify an otherwise-eligible record (overturning the 2022 Abhishek precedent); (b) requires the restitution
finding above; and (c) effective January 2026, removes stet-docket cases from public view after three years and
may make them expungement-eligible.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Tell users plainly which remedy they're getting: expungement = removed and deniable; shielding =
hidden from public but visible to police/courts and not deniable.
If a user mentions a probation violation, don't auto-disqualify them — SB 432 (2025) removed that
automatic bar. Verify the effective date for their county.

sourceSections.title: Required forms
sourceSections.text:
Maryland uses standardized Judiciary forms filed in the District or Circuit Court where the case concluded,
increasingly through MDEC e-filing.

Expungement (§ 10-105 / § 10-110)
• Petition for Expungement of Records (Judiciary form, e.g., CC-DC-CR-072 in the District Court) —
identifies the case, the disposition, the statutory basis (§ 10-105 or § 10-110), and the relief sought.
• General Waiver and Release (for § 10-105 acquittal/nolle/dismissal cases filed within 3 years) — waives
tort claims to allow earlier filing.
• Order of Expungement — the proposed order the judge signs.
• The court serves the State's Attorney with a copy and notifies any listed victims.

Shielding (§ 4-503)
• Petition for Shielding (Judiciary form) — lists the shieldable conviction(s), confirms the 3-year wait and the
once-per-lifetime condition, filed in the District or Circuit Court where the case concluded.
• Order for Shielding for the court's signature.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Pick the form by remedy and cite the right statute box (§ 10-105 for non-convictions, § 10-110 for
convictions, § 4-503 for shielding). For a within-3-year non-conviction filing, include the General Waiver
and Release to skip the wait.
File in the court where the case concluded and expect the State's Attorney to be served — build the filing
to withstand a possible objection.

Maryland Expungement & Shielding — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Filing instructions
sourceSections.text:
1 Get the record. Pull the Maryland Judiciary Case Search entry and, if needed, a CJIS criminal history to
confirm each charge, disposition, the incident grouping (for the unit rule), and sentence-completion dates.

2 Confirm the remedy and eligibility. Expungement (§ 10-105 / § 10-110) vs. shielding (§ 4-503); the
waiting period; the unit rule across the whole incident; the restitution finding; and any pending case.

3 Complete the Judiciary petition (e.g., CC-DC-CR-072) with the case details and statutory basis, plus the
proposed order; add the General Waiver and Release for an early non-conviction filing.

4 File in the District or Circuit Court where the case concluded — via MDEC e-filing where applicable —
and pay the fee ($30 for a § 10-110 conviction expungement or a shielding petition; no fee for § 10-105
non-conviction expungement).
5 Service and victim notice. The court serves the State's Attorney with a copy; for § 10-110 the court also
sends written notice to each listed victim of their right to provide information.

6 Objection / hearing. The State's Attorney may object within the statutory window; if there is an objection or
a victim responds, the court may hold a hearing, otherwise it may grant on the papers.

7 Order and execution. If granted, the court orders the agencies (and the Judiciary Case Search) to expunge or
shield the records on the statutory timeline. Keep a certified copy of the order.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Venue is the court where the case CONCLUDED (District or Circuit). A person with cases in different
counties files separately in each county.
The $30 fee applies to § 10-110 conviction expungement and to shielding; § 10-105 non-conviction
expungement is free, and unpaid court fees/costs are not a bar (the court must waive them).

Maryland Expungement & Shielding — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Court-specific quirks
sourceSections.text:
• Two remedies, different strength. Expungement removes the record (deniable); shielding only hides it from
the public (not deniable; police/courts still see it). Always name which one applies.
• The unit rule. One ineligible charge in an incident blocks expungement of every charge in that incident —
the single biggest Maryland pitfall.
• Shielding is once per lifetime and excludes domestically related convictions; it is a fallback when § 10-110
expungement is unavailable.
• REDEEM Act waits. Most misdemeanors 5 yrs; eligible felonies 7 or 10 yrs; domestic violence 15 yrs — all
from completion of the full sentence including supervision.
• SB 432 (2025). A probation violation no longer automatically disqualifies; the court must make a restitution
finding; and (from Jan 2026) stet-docket cases drop off public view after 3 years — phasing in from Oct 1,
2025.
• Clean Slate automation. Maryland is automating shielding of eligible records; a record may be shielded
without a petition once implemented — verify status.
• Court debt vs. restitution. Unpaid court fees/costs are NOT a bar (court must waive); unpaid court-ordered
restitution CAN be, unless the person cannot pay it.
• State's Attorney service + victim notice. § 10-110 petitions are served on the State's Attorney and listed
victims are notified — expect possible objection.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Lead every Maryland conviction analysis with the unit rule and the REDEEM Act waiting period; those
two determine most outcomes.
Check Clean Slate / SB 432 status for the user's situation — a record may now be eligible (or
auto-shielded) that wasn't a couple of years ago.

Maryland Expungement & Shielding — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Fee information
sourceSections.text:
Item Amount Note

§ 10-105 non-conviction expungement $0 No fee for acquittal / nolle / dismissal / PBJ / cannabis

§ 10-110 conviction expungement $30 Per petition

§ 4-503 shielding petition $30 Once per lifetime

Unpaid court fees / costs Waived Not a bar; court must waive (2023 law)

Court-ordered restitution Must be paid SB 432 (2025) finding required
or unable to pay

CJIS criminal history CJIS fee To confirm record before filing

Attorney fees (if represented) Varies Not required; MVLS and clinics offer free help

Maryland is inexpensive. Non-conviction expungement under § 10-105 is free; conviction expungement under
§ 10-110 and shielding under § 4-503 each cost $30. Since 2023, unpaid court fees and costs cannot bar
expungement and the court must waive them — but note the separate 2025 rule that court-ordered restitution
must be paid (or the person found unable to pay) before a § 10-110 expungement is granted. The Maryland
Volunteer Lawyers Service and court self-help centers assist for free.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Quote $0 for non-conviction expungement and $30 for conviction expungement or shielding; reassure
users that unpaid court fees won't block them (the court waives those).
Flag the restitution distinction: court fees are waived, but court-ordered restitution must be paid or the
person found unable to pay before a § 10-110 expungement.

sourceSections.title: Sample petitions and completed examples
sourceSections.text:
Maryland relief is filed on standardized Judiciary petitions in the court where the case concluded. The examples
below show structure and key recitals for the three remedies. Names, dates, and identifiers are fictional and
illustrative.

Example A — Petition for Expungement, conviction (§ 10-110)
IN THE DISTRICT COURT OF MARYLAND FOR BALTIMORE CITY
STATE OF MARYLAND v. ANDRE L. CARTER Case No. 1B01234567

PETITION FOR EXPUNGEMENT OF RECORDS (Crim. Proc. 10-110)
1. Petitioner: Andre L. Carter; DOB 07/22/1992.
2. On 03/14/2019 petitioner was convicted of theft under $100
(Crim. Law 7-104), an eligible misdemeanor under 10-110(a).
3. Petitioner satisfied the entire sentence -- including
probation -- on 03/14/2020.

An acquittal, nolle prosequi, dismissal, PBJ, or cannabis-
possession case is expunged at no cost. For acquittal/nolle/

A 'shieldable conviction' (one of ~12, excluding domestically
related) may be SHIELDED from public view 3 years after the
sentence is satisfied. Police and courts still see it; it is
removed from Judiciary Case Search. Fee: $30.
